narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 48% chance that you would not pass—high enough to trigger rejection as a protective measure for both you and the program. This prediction drew heavily from your overall profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 31, middle family income bracket, Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles both at 4, and your plans to attend full-time. Among the factors the model weighed most strongly, your undergraduate GPA stood out as the top influence, actually working in your favor by suggesting a stronger foundation than many who passed the bar, where the average was 3.24. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which pulled the prediction toward a higher risk of failure since 31 falls well below the 37.27 average among those who succeeded. Your middle family income bracket ranked third in influence, also nudging the model toward concern, as successful bar takers were more often from upper-middle or high-income backgrounds (45% and 9%, respectively) compared to middle (35%). Your solid Year 1 and Year 3 deciles of 4 were below the averages of 6 and 6 for passers but didn't sway the decision as much as these top three. Full-time status aligned with the norm for 93% of those who passed, so it was neutral here. Compared to typical accepted students—who tend to have higher LSATs, stronger early grades, and often more financial resources—your profile showed promise in GPA but gaps elsewhere that tipped the scales. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background; the model focuses purely on bar passage patterns from past data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, as that could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening recent academic deciles through additional coursework or exploring resources tailored for full-time students like yourself might also help. Many in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas—reapplying after targeted improvements often leads to approval.
